summary: The agent performed a comprehensive read-only analysis of the interactive config wizard codebase, producing a detailed extension point document covering the wizard entry point, prompt structure, config storage/schema, and repository function inventory. No files were modified — the output is purely a documentation/planning artifact with line-level references, function signatures, and a 10-step refactor plan for adding create/edit/delete/clone profile flows.
decisions: Recommended keeping profile management interactive flows in the CLI command layer rather than slash command handlers, due to the synchronous string-return architecture of command-handler.ts making interactive prompts infeasible
Proposed extracting wizard field definitions into a declarative WizardFieldDefinition[] data structure (Step 1) before adding profile support, to avoid duplicating prompt logic across profile flows
Decided that listProfiles() should scan both project-scoped (.orchestra/profiles/) and global-scoped (~/.orchestra/profiles/) directories and return scope-annotated results
Proposed adding readdirSync and unlinkSync to fs imports in config-repository.ts (currently missing) as prerequisites for listProfiles and deleteProfile
Recommended using atomicWrite (temp-file + rename) as the canonical write pattern for all new profile functions, consistent with saveConfigByScope
Determined that the default profile ('default') maps to the root workflow.yaml rather than profiles/default/workflow.yaml — this asymmetry is a critical invariant that new functions must respect
Proposed a 3-parameter extension to configWizardCommand options ({ cwd?, profile?, initialConfig? }) to support both create and edit profile flows without duplicating wizard logic
Recommended non-interactive slash command subcommands (profiles, clone-profile, delete-profile) with interactive create-profile redirecting to CLI, consistent with existing /config edit behavior
Flagged that command-parser.ts was NOT examined and must be reviewed before implementing new slash command kinds
Decided deleteProfile should reset the active profile preference to 'default' if the deleted profile was the currently active one

concerns.description: command-parser.ts was explicitly not examined during this analysis pass. New slash command kinds (profiles, create-profile, delete-profile, clone-profile) require parser additions, but the parser grammar and extension pattern are unknown — this is a gap in the extension point document.

The agent notes a TOCTOU race in cloneProfile: it reads source config then writes target with no lock, meaning a concurrent write to the source between read and write could produce a stale clone. The agent dismisses this as acceptable for a CLI tool but does not formally document it as a known limitation.

Unresolved question #4 flags a naming inconsistency: profiles use 'workflow.yaml' as the filename, which predates a config refactor. Any new tooling that assumes this filename is correct today could become incorrect if the filename is ever normalized. No decision was made.

The analysis confirms Zod is not used in the config layer — only the hand-rolled ConfigDiagnostic system exists. New profile functions that add validation will need to follow the same pattern or explicitly introduce Zod, which would be a style inconsistency.

The agent did not verify that the line numbers cited (e.g., L444–682 in config.ts, L112–123 in config-repository.ts) are current — they may drift if the codebase has been modified since the analysis. All implementation work should re-verify line numbers before use.

The refactor plan's Step 1 (extract WizardFieldDefinition[]) is a prerequisite for Steps 2–4 and could introduce regressions in the existing wizard if not done carefully. No test strategy is described for validating behavioral equivalence after extraction.

Unresolved question #2 (what happens to active profile preference when the active profile config file is deleted) is partially answered in Section 5d but marked as a decision point — the behavior of readActiveProfilePreference returning a name that points to a nonexistent file could cause silent fallback behavior that surprises users.

humanAttentionReason: Seven unresolved design questions require explicit decisions before implementation can begin, most critically: (1) command-parser.ts was not analyzed, blocking slash command implementation; (2) whether profile listing should show both project and global scopes or only active scope; (3) whether deleteProfile should auto-reset the active profile preference; and (4) whether createProfile should require the full wizard or support --from-defaults/--clone flags. These are business logic decisions that cannot be inferred from the codebase alone.

reviewFindings.description: command-parser.ts was not examined during the analysis pass. The extension point document is incomplete for slash command integration — parser grammar, token types, and extension pattern are undocumented.

activeProfile is captured as a const at line 451 and never re-prompted. The Step 3 refactor (change const to let and add options.profile parameter) is a required prerequisite for both create and edit profile flows — without it, the wizard always edits the currently active profile.

readdirSync and unlinkSync are not currently imported in config-repository.ts. Both are needed for listProfiles() and deleteProfile() respectively — the fs import at line 1 must be updated as part of those additions.

The DEFAULT_CONFIG_PROFILE ('default') maps to the root workflow.yaml, not profiles/default/workflow.yaml. Any new listProfiles() implementation must special-case this to avoid returning a path that doesn't exist for the default profile.

The /config edit slash command handler (lines 83–88) returns a static redirect string. If profile management non-interactive commands are added to the slash handler (profiles, clone-profile, delete-profile), the handler signature must remain synchronous — consider documenting this constraint explicitly in the handler file.

No test strategy is described for validating behavioral equivalence after the Step 1 wizard field extraction refactor. Given the wizard has 10+ prompt steps with conditional logic (agents loop, reviewer step guard), a regression test or snapshot approach should be defined before refactoring.
